#define RDB_TYPE_HASH_METADATA 24             /* Hash with HFEs. Attach min TTL at start */
 #define RDB_TYPE_HASH_LISTPACK_EX 25          /* Hash LP with HFEs. Attach min TTL at start */
 #define RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_4 26        /* Stream with IDMP support */
+#define RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_5 27        /* Stream with XNACK support (NACKed entries) */

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -79,10 +79,11 @@
 #define RDB_TYPE_HASH_METADATA 24             /* Hash with HFEs. Attach min TTL at start */
 #define RDB_TYPE_HASH_LISTPACK_EX 25          /* Hash LP with HFEs. Attach min TTL at start */
 #define RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_4 26        /* Stream with IDMP support */
+#define RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_5 27        /* Stream with XNACK support (NACKed entries) */
discussion_comments.comment_body: With the introduction of RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_5, what's the expected behavior when a newer Redis instance replicates to an older replica, or when an RDB is loaded on an older version? I'm interested in the upgrade/downgrade story and any compatibility notes users should be aware of.

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -79,10 +79,11 @@
 #define RDB_TYPE_HASH_METADATA 24             /* Hash with HFEs. Attach min TTL at start */
 #define RDB_TYPE_HASH_LISTPACK_EX 25          /* Hash LP with HFEs. Attach min TTL at start */
 #define RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_4 26        /* Stream with IDMP support */
+#define RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_5 27        /* Stream with XNACK support (NACKed entries) */
discussion_comments.comment_body: If any stream has NACKed entries, the RDB is written as type 27, which older versions will refuse to load - breaking both RDB file loading and replication to older replicas. If XNACK was never used, streams are saved as type 26, and everything stays compatible. Upgrades (old -> new) always work fine.

starting a thread unrelated to this line.
in the description you suggested META_CLASS and META_VALUES nested under a META opcode. i think we can afford to allocate each a different opcode (one more opcode).
i also think that i'd rather see all the META_CLASSes just written at the beginning of the rdb (and if we want also at the beginning of the DUMP payload), they can be completely independent of the key and its META_VALUES

Beside of the fact that we are saving opcode, it is important to pack all metadata related data into a single, logical, hierarchical, entity and explicitly define its relation to the key. Redis already treats keys as atomic units. This design extends this:
Without metadata: <TYPE, KEY, VALUE> is one unit
With metadata: <META, TYPE, KEY, VALUE> is one unit

Metadata is treated as an integral part of the key, not as separate global states. This is crucial because:
* DUMP/RESTORE: The entire key is a single, self-contained unit: <[META,] TYPE, KEY, VALUE>
* Partial RDB operations: If we ever need to serialize a subset of keys, each key carries its complete context
* Streaming/incremental loading: We can process one complete key at a time without maintaining complex global state.
* To the maintainer/reader of the code.

but if you do lazy saving of the classes, and save them just on first occurrence, you're braking this anyway.
so i'd rather just put them in the beginning of the rdb, instead of appearing to belong to the first key, but oddly missing from the next one.

Note that since you suggesting to avoid lazy saving of classes and declare it at start, unless we encode it in aux section, we won't have compatibility right from the start, even if none of the keys has metadata! This is another good reason why we should consider seriously lazy saving... and it will also save us code for DUMP/RESTORE.

i don't think the compatibility part is really important, in any case we'll increment the rdb version, so old versions won't be able to load new rdb files.
i'm more interested about a new version with and without a certain module, but we'll be able to handle that either way.

i won't insist on saving them at the start, it just seems more logical to me not to save global data inside the section that saves individual keys.
i agree it'll inflict some complexity for DUMP, but i'm sure we can solve it.

your call.
